title: Gazpacho
subtitle: Raw Andalusian soup, sharp with vinegar, served cold.
excerpt: Ripe tomatoes blended with cucumber, red pepper, garlic, sherry vinegar and olive oil into a chilled soup of startling freshness. No cooking at all. Serve in bowls with a float of extra-virgin olive oil and a scattering of chopped cucumber and croutons. Feeds four.
sourceNotes: Traditional Andalusian gazpacho; the vinegar-and-olive-oil balance draws on the classic Córdoba proportions documented in Luisa Osborne's pre-1928 household papers and later formalised by Simone Ortega in Mil Ochenta Recetas de Cocina (1972, widely cited as the canonical 20th-century reference). The USDA produce safety guidance for raw vegetable preparations informs the storage window.

content.text: Everything raw. Ripe tomatoes, cucumber, red pepper, a little garlic, sherry vinegar, olive oil, salt. Blended together and chilled until the flavours settle into something sharper and colder than any of its parts. No cooking, no heat at all. The depth comes from good tomatoes and the overnight rest in the fridge.

The Andalusian summer version is the one most people know: smooth, vinegar-bright, served very cold in shallow bowls with a pool of oil on top. The ingredient proportions matter less than the quality of the tomatoes. A pale, flavourless tomato makes a pale, flavourless gazpacho; a deep-red, sun-warm one makes something worth eating in August.

items.name: Cucumber
items.prepNote: peeled, deseeded, roughly chopped, reserve a third for garnish

content.text: Put {{tomato}} of chopped tomatoes, two-thirds of the chopped {{cucumber}} cucumber, {{pepper-red}} red pepper, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{sherry-vinegar}} of sherry vinegar, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt into a blender. If using bread, add the torn slices now. Blend on high for 90 seconds until completely smooth.

With the blender running on low, pour {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of oil in a slow, steady stream through the feed tube. The soup will lighten in colour and thicken slightly as the oil emulsifies. Blend for another 30 seconds on high.

Pass through a fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl or jug, pressing the pulp to extract all the liquid. Taste and adjust salt and vinegar. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours; overnight is better.
Ladle the cold soup into shallow bowls. Add a pool of extra-virgin olive oil on top. Scatter with the reserved finely diced cucumber and any croutons or chopped herbs you like.

items.fix: Use the ripest, most flavourful tomatoes available. Reduce cucumber slightly. A pinch more salt and another splash of vinegar often rescues it.
items.cause: Tomatoes lacked flavour or too much cucumber was used.

items.fix: Start with half a clove next time. Rub the blender bowl with the cut side of garlic rather than adding it whole for a subtler result.
items.cause: Too much garlic, or the garlic was old.

items.fix: Normal. Re-blend briefly for 30 seconds before serving. Bread as a thickener helps maintain the emulsion; add a slice next time.
items.cause: The oil emulsion broke, or the bread was omitted.

items.fix: Chill the bowls as well as the soup. Serve over one or two ice cubes if needed.

content.text: Ajo blanco is the pale Málaga cousin: blanched almonds replace the tomatoes and vegetables. Salmorejo, from Córdoba, is thicker and richer, with more bread and a tighter ratio of tomato to oil, served with a garnish of hard-boiled egg and jamón. Both are worth knowing.

Gazpacho is the cold soup of Andalusia: a field worker's meal in the heat of a Spanish summer, built from the bread and raw vegetables that were on hand. The tomato version is relatively modern; the original was a white garlic bread soup that pre-dates the tomato's arrival in Spain. Today the red version is the standard, and it belongs at any summer table where the heat makes cooking feel wrong.
